content.full_text:
State of California                                           m- 83-066

Memorandum

To      : Advice Request Meeting Participants              Date  : 2/3/83
         Bob Stern


From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Jeanne

Subject: Reporting dates of contributions made


        Is the "date a contribution is made" the date the
contribution is authorized or the date the check is
issued?

        I have been getting calls from corporations which
file major donor campaign statements expressing horror
and trepidation that they are now going to be required
to report the dates of contributions.

        It is a problem because they have computerized
accounting and billing systems, and the date the check
is issued may differ from the date the contribution is
authorized. The person who authorizes the contribution
(and who prepares the campaign statements) never knows
when the check is actually issued. The check may be
issued anywhere from 24 hours to a month and a half after
the authorization.

        It seems to me that at the time the contribution
is authorized the company has made a decision to make an
expenditure and has, in effect, committed the funds,
and that is the date the contribution has been made.


CONCLUSION: It is permissible for committees to use the authorization
            date as the date of contribution. I checked with Helen
            Fong at PRAD, and she indicated that so long as it is
            clear from the committee's records that the date used is
            the authorization date, rather than the date of the
            check, it will not present an auditing problem.

sections.question: Is the "date a contribution is made" the date the contribution is authorized or the date the check is issued?
sections.conclusion:
It is permissible for committees to use the authorization 
date as the date of contribution. 
I checked with Helen 
Fong at PRAD, and she indicated that so long as it is 
clear from the committee's records that the date used is 
the authorization date, rather than the date of the 
check, it will not present an auditing problem.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether for campaign finance reporting purposes, the date a contribution is made should be the date it is authorized or the date the check is issued.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Committees may use the authorization date as the contribution date rather than the check issuance date, provided that the committee's records clearly identify that the authorization date was used for reporting purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document appears to be a memorandum-style response from Jeanne at FPPC to recipient regarding contribution reporting dates.

I have been getting calls from corporations which file major donor campaign statements expressing horror and trepidation that they are now going to be required to report the dates of contributions. It is a problem because they have computerized accounting and billing systems, and the date the check is issued may differ from the date the contribution is authorized. The person who authorizes the contribution (and who prepares the campaign statements) never knows when the check is actually issued. The check may be issued anywhere from 24 hours to a month and a half after the authorization. It seems to me that at the time the contribution is authorized the company has made a decision to make an expenditure and has, in effect, committed the funds, and that is the date the contribution has been made. CONCLUSION: It is permissible for committees to use the authorization date as the date of contribution. I checked with Helen Fong at PRAD, and she indicated that so long as it is clear from the committee's records that the date used is the authorization date, rather than the date of the check, it will not present an auditing problem.
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to questions from corporations about reporting dates for campaign contributions, clarifying that the authorization date may be used instead of the check issuance date.
